attributes.stem:
A 14-month-old boy presents to the General Practice clinic with his parents due to concerns about developmental delay. He has not yet started walking and has limited vocabulary. His parents report that he has been irritable and lethargic for the past few weeks. He is exclusively breastfed by his vegan mother. He has no significant medical history and takes no regular medications. On examination, he appears pale with vital signs within normal limits. Neurological examination reveals hypotonia and decreased deep tendon reflexes.

Investigations reveal:

Haemoglobin: 82 g/L (105-135)
Mean corpuscular volume (MCV): 102 fL (70-86)
White cell count: 3.8 x 10^9/L (6.0-17.5)
Platelet count: 110 x 10^9/L (150-400)
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate next investigation?
attributes.explanation:
This case presents a 14-month-old boy with developmental delay, neurological symptoms, and haematological findings consistent with macrocytic anaemia. The most appropriate next investigation is a serum vitamin B12 level.

Several factors in this case point towards vitamin B12 deficiency as the likely cause of the child's symptoms and macrocytic anaemia:

1. Developmental delay and limited vocabulary suggest neurological involvement, which is common in vitamin B12 deficiency.

2. The child is exclusively breastfed by a vegan mother, putting him at high risk for B12 deficiency as plant-based diets are naturally low in this vitamin.

3. The blood results show macrocytic anaemia (low haemoglobin with high MCV) along with pancytopenia (low white cell and platelet counts), which are characteristic of vitamin B12 deficiency.

According to the study by Sarbay and Ay (2018), vitamin B12 deficiency was the most common cause of macrocytosis in children, accounting for 38.7% of cases. The study also found that neurological symptoms were present in 33.3% of children with vitamin B12 deficiency.

Serum folate level could be considered as folate deficiency can also cause macrocytic anaemia. However, neurological symptoms are more typical of B12 deficiency, and the dietary history (vegan mother) makes B12 deficiency more likely.

Serum ferritin level is not the most appropriate next step as iron deficiency typically causes microcytic anaemia, not macrocytic anaemia as seen in this case.

Haemoglobin electrophoresis is used to diagnose haemoglobinopathies such as thalassaemia, which typically cause microcytic anaemia and are not consistent with this clinical picture.

Bone marrow aspiration is an invasive procedure that would not be the next step in this case. It might be considered if the diagnosis remains unclear after less invasive investigations.

After confirming vitamin B12 deficiency with a serum level, additional tests such as methylmalonic acid and homocysteine levels can help confirm the diagnosis. Treatment typically involves intramuscular vitamin B12 injections, with oral supplements for the mother if she chooses to continue breastfeeding. Dietary counselling for the family would also be important to prevent recurrence.
learningPoints: Vitamin B12 deficiency is a common cause of macrocytic anaemia in children with developmental delay and neurological symptoms.
Serum vitamin B12 levels, methylmalonic acid, and homocysteine are important investigations in diagnosing vitamin B12 deficiency.
Intramuscular vitamin B12 injections are the preferred initial treatment for severe vitamin B12 deficiency in children.
references: Sarbay H, Ay Y. Evaluation of children with macrocytosis: clinical study. Pan Afr Med J. 2018 Sep 25;31:54. French. doi: 10.11604/pamj.2018.31.54.15498. PMID: 30923599; PMCID: PMC6431413.

Moore CA, Adil A. Macrocytic Anemia. [Updated 2022 Jul 11]. In: StatPearls [Internet]. Treasure Island (FL): StatPearls Publishing; 2024 Jan-. Available from: https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK459295/

attributes.description: Apply, analyze, evaluate, and create (implementing, organizing, and critiquing information)
